roadmap.title: 45-Day AI Engineer Roadmap
roadmap.description: A structured 45-day beginner curriculum to become an AI Engineer, covering programming, data handling, math foundations, machine learning, deep learning, NLP, LLMs, model deployment, and MLOps to prepare students for entry-level AI Engineering roles.
roadmap.outcome: Ability to build, train, evaluate, and deploy machine learning and AI models into production applications.

days.title: The AI Engineer Landscape and Role
objectives: Understand what an AI Engineer does
Differentiate AI, ML, DL, and Data Science roles
Map the skills and tools you will learn
topics: AI vs ML vs Deep Learning vs Data Science
days.beginner_explanation: An AI Engineer sits between data science and software engineering: they build, train, and ship AI models into real applications. Today you learn how AI, ML, deep learning, and data science relate, what an AI Engineer actually does day to day, and the end-to-end lifecycle of an AI project from data to deployment. This mental map guides everything you learn over the next 45 days.
days.practical_task: Create a diagram of the AI/ML project lifecycle and label which tools apply at each stage.
days.mini_project: Research three real AI Engineer job postings and produce a skills-gap analysis comparing them to this roadmap.
days.assignment: Write a one-page personal learning plan defining your target role, goals, and how you will track progress.

quiz.question: Which best describes an AI Engineer's focus?
options: Building and deploying AI models into applications
quiz.answer: Building and deploying AI models into applications

quiz.question: Deep learning is a subset of...
The first stage of the ML lifecycle is usually...

youtube.title: AI vs Machine Learning vs Deep Learning
How to Become an AI Engineer

research_papers.title: A Few Useful Things to Know About Machine Learning (Domingos, 2012)

days.expected_outcome: You understand the AI Engineer role and have a clear personal learning plan.

days.title: Python Fundamentals for AI
days.beginner_explanation: Python is the language of AI. Its readable syntax and vast library ecosystem make it the default for machine learning. Today you learn the core building blocks: storing data in variables, controlling flow with conditionals and loops, and packaging logic into functions. You also set up isolated virtual environments so each project's dependencies stay clean and reproducible.
days.practical_task: Set up Python and a virtual environment, then write a script that classifies numbers as prime using a function and a loop.
days.mini_project: Build a command-line grade calculator that takes scores, computes averages and letter grades, and handles invalid input.
days.assignment: Write five small programs (temperature converter, FizzBuzz, factorial, palindrome check, simple calculator) and push them to GitHub.

quiz.question: Which keyword defines a function in Python?
A virtual environment is used to...
Which loop repeats while a condition is true?

youtube.title: Python for Beginners - Learn Python in 1 Hour

research_papers.title: Python for Scientific Computing (Oliphant, 2007)

days.expected_outcome: You can write basic Python programs and manage project environments.

days.title: Python Data Structures and OOP
days.beginner_explanation: Data structures determine how you store and access information efficiently, while object-oriented programming lets you model real-world entities as reusable objects with data and behavior. Most AI libraries are built with OOP, so understanding classes, inheritance, and exception handling helps you both use those libraries and write clean, maintainable AI code.
days.practical_task: Create a Dataset class that stores records in a list of dictionaries and offers add, filter, and summary methods with error handling.
days.mini_project: Build a mini inventory system with Product and Inventory classes that reads from and writes to a JSON file.
days.assignment: Refactor Day 2's grade calculator into an OOP design with a Student class and exception handling.

quiz.question: Which data structure is immutable?
What does 'self' refer to in a method?
Which structure stores unique unordered items?

research_papers.title: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software (overview)

days.expected_outcome: You can model problems with classes and choose appropriate data structures.

days.title: Git, GitHub, and Developer Workflow
days.beginner_explanation: Version control is a non-negotiable skill for any engineer. Git tracks every change to your code so you can experiment safely, revert mistakes, and collaborate without overwriting others' work. GitHub hosts your repositories and is also your public portfolio. Employers look at your GitHub, so learning a clean commit and branching workflow early pays off throughout your career.
days.practical_task: Initialize a Git repo, make several commits on a feature branch, and merge it into main, resolving a deliberate conflict.
days.mini_project: Publish your previous projects to a well-organized GitHub profile with READMEs and a .gitignore for Python.
days.assignment: Fork an open-source repo, make a small improvement, and open a pull request following its contribution guidelines.

quiz.question: Which command saves a snapshot of staged changes?
A branch is used to...
A .gitignore file specifies...

youtube.title: Git and GitHub for Beginners - Crash Course

research_papers.title: A Framework for Distributed Version Control (overview)

days.expected_outcome: You can version-control code and collaborate professionally on GitHub.

days.title: NumPy for Numerical Computing
days.beginner_explanation: NumPy is the foundation of scientific Python. It provides fast, memory-efficient arrays and vectorized operations implemented in C, so you avoid slow Python loops. Every ML dataset, image, and model weight is ultimately a NumPy-style array, making this the essential first step before machine learning and deep learning.
days.practical_task: Create a random 5x5 matrix, compute row and column means, normalize it, and locate the maximum value's index.
days.mini_project: Build a grayscale image editor using NumPy arrays to brighten, invert, and crop an image.
days.assignment: Reimplement dot product, matrix multiplication, and mean squared error using only NumPy, without Python loops.

quiz.question: Broadcasting enables...
options: Operations on arrays of different shapes
quiz.answer: Operations on arrays of different shapes

quiz.question: Which creates an array of zeros?
In a 2D array, axis=0 refers to...

research_papers.title: Array programming with NumPy (Harris et al., Nature 2020)

days.expected_outcome: You can manipulate numerical data efficiently with NumPy.

days.title: Pandas for Data Handling
days.beginner_explanation: Pandas is the workhorse for loading, cleaning, and exploring tabular data, and data preparation is where AI engineers spend most of their time. DataFrames give you a spreadsheet-like interface backed by NumPy speed. Mastering filtering, missing-value handling, grouping, and joins lets you turn raw, messy data into model-ready datasets.
days.practical_task: Load the Titanic dataset, handle missing values, and compute survival rates grouped by passenger class and gender.
days.mini_project: Build a data-cleaning pipeline that standardizes columns, fills missing values, removes duplicates, and outputs a summary report.
days.assignment: Pick a public dataset and produce five insights using groupby, filtering, and merging in a Jupyter notebook.

quiz.question: Which method shows the first rows?
Which method fills missing values?
groupby is used to...
options: Split data into groups for aggregation
quiz.answer: Split data into groups for aggregation

research_papers.title: Data Structures for Statistical Computing in Python (McKinney, 2010)

days.expected_outcome: You can load, clean, and analyze real-world tabular datasets.

days.title: Data Visualization
days.beginner_explanation: Visualization turns numbers into understanding. AI engineers constantly inspect data distributions, correlations, and model results. Matplotlib gives you fine-grained control over every element of a chart, while Seaborn produces polished statistical visualizations with little code. Clear charts help you find data problems and communicate findings to stakeholders.
days.practical_task: For a dataset, create a histogram, a scatter plot with a trend line, and a correlation heatmap, then interpret each.
days.mini_project: Build an exploratory data analysis notebook with at least six visualizations and written insights for a dataset.
days.assignment: Recreate three chart types from a data-journalism article and explain what makes each effective.

quiz.question: Which library is built on Matplotlib for statistics?
Which plot shows a single variable's distribution?
A correlation heatmap displays...

research_papers.title: Matplotlib: A 2D Graphics Environment (Hunter, 2007)

days.expected_outcome: You can visualize data and communicate insights through charts.

days.title: SQL for AI and Data
days.beginner_explanation: Most real-world data lives in databases, so SQL is essential for AI engineers who need to fetch and shape training data. You learn to select, filter, aggregate, and join tables to produce clean datasets, then pull query results directly into pandas for modeling. SQL fluency also appears in almost every AI Engineer interview.
days.practical_task: Write SQL queries to join two tables, aggregate results with GROUP BY, and filter with a subquery, then load them into pandas.
days.mini_project: Design a small SQLite database for an app, populate it, and write analytical queries to produce a dataset for ML.
days.assignment: Solve 15 SQL exercises covering joins, aggregation, and subqueries on a sample database.

quiz.question: Which clause filters rows?
A JOIN combines...
options: Rows from multiple tables by keys
quiz.answer: Rows from multiple tables by keys

quiz.question: GROUP BY is used with...

youtube.title: SQL Tutorial - Full Database Course for Beginners

research_papers.title: A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks (Codd, 1970)

days.expected_outcome: You can extract and shape datasets from relational databases with SQL.

days.title: Linear Algebra for Machine Learning
objectives: Grasp eigenvalues, eigenvectors, and their ML relevance
days.beginner_explanation: Linear algebra is the language of machine learning. Data is represented as vectors and matrices, and nearly every ML operation - from a neural network layer to a recommendation engine - is a series of matrix multiplications. Understanding how these structures behave lets you reason about model shapes, dimensions, and transformations instead of treating them as black boxes.
days.practical_task: Use NumPy to create matrices, multiply them, compute transposes and inverses, and calculate eigenvalues/eigenvectors of a 2x2 matrix.
days.mini_project: Build a simple image transformation tool that applies matrix operations (rotation, scaling) to a small grayscale image represented as a NumPy array.
days.assignment: Given two matrices, compute their product by hand, then verify with NumPy. Explain why matrix multiplication is not commutative.

quiz.question: For matrix multiplication A*B to be valid, what must be true?
What does an eigenvector of a matrix represent?
options: A vector whose direction is unchanged under the transformation
quiz.answer: A vector whose direction is unchanged under the transformation

research_papers.title: A Singularly Valuable Decomposition: The SVD of a Matrix

days.expected_outcome: You can manipulate vectors and matrices and understand their role in ML computations.

days.title: Calculus and Optimization for ML
objectives: Grasp the chain rule for backpropagation
Learn how gradient descent optimizes models
days.beginner_explanation: Training a model means minimizing a loss function - finding the parameter values that make predictions as accurate as possible. Calculus gives us gradients, which tell us the direction of steepest change. Gradient descent uses these gradients to iteratively nudge model parameters toward lower loss. This is the engine behind almost all model training.
days.practical_task: Implement gradient descent from scratch in Python to minimize a simple quadratic function, and plot how the value converges over iterations.
days.mini_project: Build a linear regression trainer using manual gradient descent (no scikit-learn) on a small dataset and visualize the fitted line improving each epoch.
days.assignment: Experiment with three different learning rates on your gradient descent implementation. Document what happens with a rate that is too high, too low, and just right.

quiz.question: What does the gradient of a loss function tell you?
What happens if the learning rate is too high?
options: The model may overshoot and diverge
quiz.answer: The model may overshoot and diverge

research_papers.title: An Overview of Gradient Descent Optimization Algorithms (Ruder, 2016)

days.expected_outcome: You understand how models learn by minimizing loss through gradient descent.

days.title: Probability and Statistics for AI
objectives: Grasp Bayes' theorem and its ML uses
days.beginner_explanation: Machine learning is fundamentally about reasoning under uncertainty. Statistics helps you understand your data's distribution, spot patterns, and quantify confidence. Probability underpins models like Naive Bayes and concepts like likelihood. Knowing these fundamentals prevents common mistakes such as confusing correlation with causation or misreading noisy results.
days.practical_task: Use NumPy and SciPy to generate samples from normal and binomial distributions, compute summary statistics, and visualize histograms.
days.mini_project: Build a Naive Bayes spam classifier from scratch using probability to classify short text messages as spam or ham.
days.assignment: Given a medical test with known accuracy, use Bayes' theorem to compute the probability that a positive result is a true positive. Explain the intuition.

quiz.question: Bayes' theorem lets you compute what?
options: A posterior probability from a prior and likelihood
quiz.answer: A posterior probability from a prior and likelihood

quiz.question: Which statement is true?

youtube.title: Statistics - A Full University Course on Data Science Basics

research_papers.title: An Essay towards solving a Problem in the Doctrine of Chances (Bayes, 1763)

days.expected_outcome: You can analyze data statistically and reason about uncertainty in models.

days.title: Data Cleaning and Feature Engineering
days.beginner_explanation: Real-world data is messy - full of gaps, inconsistencies, and inappropriate formats. Feature engineering is the art of transforming raw data into inputs that models can learn from effectively. It is often said that data preparation determines model success more than the choice of algorithm. Avoiding data leakage - where test information sneaks into training - is critical for trustworthy results.
days.practical_task: Take a messy dataset (e.g., Titanic), handle missing values, encode categoricals, scale numeric features, and prepare a clean train/test split.
days.mini_project: Build a reusable data preprocessing pipeline that ingests raw CSV data and outputs a model-ready cleaned dataset with documented transformations.
days.assignment: Explain what data leakage is with a concrete example, and describe how scaling must be fit only on training data to avoid it.

quiz.question: Why should feature scaling be fit only on training data?
options: To avoid data leakage from the test set
quiz.answer: To avoid data leakage from the test set

quiz.question: One-hot encoding is used for what?
options: Converting categorical variables into binary columns
quiz.answer: Converting categorical variables into binary columns

research_papers.title: A Few Useful Things to Know About Machine Learning (Domingos, 2012)

days.expected_outcome: You can transform messy raw data into clean, model-ready features.

days.title: Introduction to Machine Learning
objectives: Understand the ML workflow end to end
Distinguish supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning
topics: Supervised vs unsupervised vs reinforcement learning
The ML workflow (data -> train -> evaluate -> deploy)
days.beginner_explanation: Machine learning enables computers to learn patterns from data rather than being explicitly programmed. Before diving into algorithms, it is vital to understand the overall workflow and core concepts like generalization - a model's ability to perform well on unseen data. The bias-variance tradeoff explains the central tension in ML: too simple a model underfits, too complex a model overfits.
days.practical_task: Train your first model with scikit-learn on a built-in dataset (iris), then evaluate its accuracy on a held-out test set.
days.mini_project: Create a comparison notebook that trains the same dataset with an underfit, well-fit, and overfit model, and visualizes the difference in train vs test performance.
days.assignment: In your own words, explain the bias-variance tradeoff and give a real-world analogy for overfitting.

quiz.question: Overfitting means a model:
options: Memorizes training data but fails on new data
quiz.answer: Memorizes training data but fails on new data

quiz.question: Which is an example of supervised learning?
options: Predicting house prices from labeled data
quiz.answer: Predicting house prices from labeled data

youtube.title: Machine Learning for Everybody - Full Course

research_papers.title: Statistical Modeling: The Two Cultures (Breiman, 2001)

days.expected_outcome: You understand the ML lifecycle and can train and evaluate a basic model.

days.title: Regression Algorithms
days.beginner_explanation: Regression predicts continuous values - like prices, temperatures, or scores. Linear regression is the foundational algorithm and a great way to understand how models fit data by minimizing error. Regularization techniques like Ridge and Lasso prevent overfitting by penalizing large coefficients, and choosing the right evaluation metric ensures you measure what actually matters for your problem.
days.practical_task: Train linear, polynomial, and Ridge regression models on a housing dataset and compare their RMSE and R2 scores.
days.mini_project: Build a house price prediction model with feature engineering and regularization, and report which features most influence price.
days.assignment: Compare Ridge and Lasso: explain how each handles coefficients and when you would choose one over the other.

quiz.question: Which metric penalizes large errors more heavily?
Lasso regularization can do what that Ridge cannot?
options: Drive some coefficients to exactly zero
quiz.answer: Drive some coefficients to exactly zero

research_papers.title: Regression Shrinkage and Selection via the Lasso (Tibshirani, 1996)

days.expected_outcome: You can build and evaluate regression models with regularization.

days.title: Classification Algorithms
objectives: Understand logistic regression and decision trees
days.beginner_explanation: Classification predicts categories - spam or not, disease or healthy, which digit. Different algorithms make different assumptions: logistic regression draws linear boundaries, decision trees split on features, KNN uses proximity, and SVMs find optimal margins. Crucially, accuracy alone can mislead on imbalanced data, so precision, recall, and F1 give a fuller picture of performance.
days.practical_task: Train logistic regression, a decision tree, KNN, and SVM on a classification dataset and compare their precision, recall, and F1 scores.
days.mini_project: Build a customer churn predictor, handle class imbalance, and produce a confusion matrix and ROC curve to justify the chosen model.
days.assignment: Explain the difference between precision and recall and give a scenario where recall matters more than precision.

quiz.question: In a cancer screening test, which metric is usually most important?
What does the confusion matrix show?

research_papers.title: Support-Vector Networks (Cortes & Vapnik, 1995)

days.expected_outcome: You can build classifiers and evaluate them with appropriate metrics.

days.title: Unsupervised Learning
topics: Choosing the number of clusters (elbow, silhouette)
days.beginner_explanation: Unsupervised learning finds structure in unlabeled data. Clustering groups similar data points - useful for customer segmentation or anomaly detection. Dimensionality reduction like PCA compresses many features into a few while retaining most information, which helps visualization and speeds up other models. Since there are no labels, evaluating results requires different techniques like the silhouette score.
days.practical_task: Apply K-Means to a customer dataset, use the elbow method to pick k, then use PCA to visualize the clusters in 2D.
days.mini_project: Build a customer segmentation tool that clusters shoppers by behavior and profiles each segment for a marketing team.
days.assignment: Explain how PCA reduces dimensionality and what information is lost. When would you use it before training a model?

quiz.question: K-Means requires you to specify what in advance?
PCA is primarily used for:

research_papers.title: A Density-Based Algorithm for Discovering Clusters (DBSCAN, Ester et al., 1996)

days.expected_outcome: You can discover structure in unlabeled data and reduce dimensions with PCA.

days.title: Scikit-learn Pipelines and Workflow
days.beginner_explanation: As projects grow, ad-hoc scripts become error-prone. Pipelines bundle preprocessing and modeling into a single reproducible object, ensuring the same transformations apply consistently and preventing data leakage. Cross-validation gives a more reliable performance estimate than a single split, and systematic hyperparameter tuning helps you find the best model configuration instead of guessing.
days.practical_task: Build a scikit-learn Pipeline combining preprocessing and a classifier, then use GridSearchCV with cross-validation to tune hyperparameters.
days.mini_project: Create an end-to-end reproducible ML pipeline that loads data, preprocesses, tunes, evaluates via cross-validation, and saves the best model to disk.
days.assignment: Explain why wrapping preprocessing inside a pipeline (rather than doing it separately) prevents data leakage during cross-validation.

quiz.question: What is the main benefit of cross-validation over a single train/test split?
GridSearchCV is used to:

research_papers.title: Scikit-learn: Machine Learning in Python (Pedregosa et al., 2011)

days.expected_outcome: You can build reproducible, leak-free ML pipelines with tuned hyperparameters.

days.title: Model Evaluation and Validation
objectives: Master evaluation metrics for different tasks
Diagnose model problems with learning curves
days.beginner_explanation: Choosing and interpreting the right evaluation strategy separates a reliable model from a misleading one. Different problems demand different metrics, and validation techniques ensure your performance estimates generalize. Learning curves reveal whether more data would help or whether your model is fundamentally over/underfitting, guiding your next steps intelligently rather than by trial and error.
days.practical_task: Plot learning curves for a model to diagnose overfitting vs underfitting, and apply a technique to handle a class-imbalanced dataset.
days.mini_project: Build an evaluation report generator that, given a trained model and dataset, outputs all relevant metrics, curves, and a plain-language diagnosis.
days.assignment: Given a learning curve showing high training accuracy but low validation accuracy, diagnose the problem and propose three fixes.

quiz.question: A large gap between high training accuracy and low validation accuracy indicates:
SMOTE is used to address:

research_papers.title: SMOTE: Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (Chawla et al., 2002)

days.expected_outcome: You can rigorously evaluate models and diagnose performance problems.

days.title: Ensemble Methods: Random Forests and Gradient Boosting
days.beginner_explanation: Ensemble methods combine many weak models into one strong predictor and dominate tabular data competitions. Random Forests average many decision trees (bagging) to reduce variance, while gradient boosting builds trees sequentially, each correcting the previous one's errors. XGBoost and LightGBM are the go-to tools for structured data problems in industry due to their accuracy and speed.
days.practical_task: Train a Random Forest and an XGBoost model on the same dataset, compare performance, and plot feature importances.
days.mini_project: Build a credit-risk or loan-default predictor using XGBoost, tune it, and produce a feature-importance report explaining the top drivers.
days.assignment: Explain the difference between bagging and boosting, and why boosting can overfit if not regularized.

quiz.question: Random Forests reduce overfitting mainly by:
Gradient boosting builds trees:

research_papers.title: XGBoost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System (Chen & Guestrin, 2016)

days.expected_outcome: You can build high-performing ensemble models for tabular data.

days.title: Feature Selection and Dimensionality Reduction
days.beginner_explanation: More features are not always better - irrelevant or redundant features add noise, slow training, and can hurt accuracy. Feature selection keeps only what matters, while dimensionality reduction transforms features into a compact representation. Techniques like t-SNE and UMAP help you visualize high-dimensional data in 2D, revealing clusters and structure that would otherwise be invisible.
days.practical_task: Apply recursive feature elimination and PCA to a high-dimensional dataset, then visualize it with t-SNE.
days.mini_project: Build a feature-selection toolkit that ranks features by multiple methods and reports the smallest feature set that retains most accuracy.
days.assignment: Explain the curse of dimensionality with an example and describe two strategies to mitigate it.

quiz.question: t-SNE is primarily used for:
The curse of dimensionality refers to:
options: Problems arising as feature count grows very large
quiz.answer: Problems arising as feature count grows very large

research_papers.title: Visualizing Data using t-SNE (van der Maaten & Hinton, 2008)

days.expected_outcome: You can reduce feature space intelligently to improve models and insight.

days.title: Introduction to Neural Networks
days.beginner_explanation: Neural networks are inspired by the brain: layers of simple units (neurons) each compute a weighted sum and apply an activation function to introduce non-linearity. Stacking layers lets networks learn increasingly abstract representations - edges, then shapes, then objects in an image. Understanding forward propagation - how input flows to output - is the first step before learning how networks train via backpropagation.
days.practical_task: Implement a single neuron and a small 2-layer network from scratch in NumPy, computing the forward pass by hand.
days.mini_project: Build a from-scratch neural network (NumPy only) that learns the XOR function, demonstrating why non-linear activations are needed.
days.assignment: Explain why activation functions must be non-linear, and what happens if you stack only linear layers.

quiz.question: Why do neural networks need non-linear activation functions?
options: So stacked layers can model non-linear relationships
quiz.answer: So stacked layers can model non-linear relationships

quiz.question: ReLU outputs:

youtube.title: But what is a neural network?

research_papers.title: Learning representations by back-propagating errors (Rumelhart et al., 1986)

days.expected_outcome: You understand how neural networks are structured and make predictions.

days.title: Deep Learning Fundamentals and Backpropagation
days.beginner_explanation: Backpropagation is the algorithm that makes deep learning possible - it efficiently computes how much each weight contributed to the error using the chain rule, then updates weights to reduce loss. Beyond the core algorithm, practical training relies on optimizers like Adam, regularization like dropout to prevent overfitting, and techniques like batch normalization to stabilize and speed up learning.
days.practical_task: Extend your from-scratch network to implement backpropagation, then train it on a small dataset and watch the loss decrease.
days.mini_project: Build a configurable neural network trainer (NumPy) supporting different optimizers and regularization, and compare their convergence.
days.assignment: Explain the vanishing gradient problem and describe two techniques that help mitigate it.

quiz.question: Backpropagation uses which mathematical rule to compute gradients?
Dropout helps by:
options: Randomly deactivating neurons to reduce overfitting
quiz.answer: Randomly deactivating neurons to reduce overfitting

research_papers.title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization (Kingma & Ba, 2014)

days.expected_outcome: You understand how deep networks train and how to stabilize training.

days.title: PyTorch Basics
objectives: Build and train networks in PyTorch
days.beginner_explanation: PyTorch is the most popular deep learning framework for research and increasingly for production. It provides tensors (like NumPy arrays but GPU-accelerated) and autograd, which automatically computes gradients so you do not have to hand-code backpropagation. Learning to write clean training loops with nn.Module, DataLoaders, and optimizers is a core skill for any AI engineer.
days.practical_task: Rebuild a neural network in PyTorch, define a training loop, and train it on MNIST or a similar dataset to reach good accuracy.
days.mini_project: Build an image classifier in PyTorch with a custom Dataset/DataLoader, training loop, validation, and model checkpointing.
days.assignment: Explain what autograd does and how requires_grad and .backward() work together in a training step.

quiz.question: In PyTorch, what does loss.backward() do?
What is the role of an optimizer.step()?

youtube.title: PyTorch for Deep Learning & Machine Learning - Full Course

research_papers.title: PyTorch: An Imperative Style, High-Performance Deep Learning Library (Paszke et al., 2019)

days.expected_outcome: You can build and train neural networks end to end in PyTorch.

days.title: TensorFlow and Keras
objectives: Build models with the Keras API
days.beginner_explanation: TensorFlow with Keras is the other major deep learning framework, widely used in production and on mobile/edge via TensorFlow Lite. Keras offers a clean, high-level API that lets you build and train models with very little code. Knowing both PyTorch and TensorFlow at a basic level makes you flexible, since teams and job postings vary in which they use.
days.practical_task: Build the same image classifier from the PyTorch day using Keras, using callbacks for early stopping and checkpointing.
days.mini_project: Build a Keras model for a tabular or image dataset, add callbacks, save the model, and reload it for inference.
days.assignment: Compare PyTorch and TensorFlow/Keras in terms of API style, and describe a scenario where you might pick each.

quiz.question: The Keras Sequential API is best for:
An EarlyStopping callback helps by:
options: Halting training when validation stops improving
quiz.answer: Halting training when validation stops improving

research_papers.title: TensorFlow: A System for Large-Scale Machine Learning (Abadi et al., 2016)

days.expected_outcome: You can build and train models with Keras and understand the TF ecosystem.

days.title: Convolutional Neural Networks and Computer Vision
days.beginner_explanation: Convolutional Neural Networks revolutionized computer vision by learning spatial features automatically. Convolution layers slide small filters over images to detect patterns like edges and textures, while pooling reduces spatial size and adds robustness. Modern architectures like ResNet stack many layers with skip connections. Data augmentation artificially expands your dataset, improving generalization for image tasks.
days.practical_task: Build and train a CNN in PyTorch or Keras on CIFAR-10 with data augmentation, and evaluate its accuracy.
days.mini_project: Build an image classifier (e.g., cats vs dogs, or plant disease detection) with a CNN and augmentation, and report performance.
days.assignment: Explain how a convolution layer differs from a fully connected layer and why it is efficient for images.

quiz.question: What does a pooling layer do?
options: Downsamples feature maps for robustness and efficiency
quiz.answer: Downsamples feature maps for robustness and efficiency

quiz.question: ResNet introduced which key idea?

research_papers.title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition (He et al., 2015)

days.expected_outcome: You can build CNNs to solve image classification problems.

days.title: Recurrent Networks and Sequence Models
objectives: Recognize limitations that led to transformers
topics: Time series and text as sequences
days.beginner_explanation: Many problems involve sequences - text, speech, time series - where order matters. Recurrent networks process sequences step by step, maintaining a hidden state that carries information forward. LSTMs and GRUs add gating to remember long-range dependencies. Understanding RNNs and their limitations (difficulty with long sequences) sets the stage for appreciating why transformers and attention took over.
days.practical_task: Build an LSTM in PyTorch or Keras to forecast a simple time series or classify text sentiment.
days.mini_project: Build a text sentiment classifier or a stock/weather time-series forecaster using an LSTM, and evaluate it.
days.assignment: Explain how an LSTM's gates help it remember long-range dependencies compared to a vanilla RNN.

quiz.question: What problem do LSTMs address compared to vanilla RNNs?
RNNs process sequences:
options: Step by step maintaining a hidden state
quiz.answer: Step by step maintaining a hidden state

youtube.title: Illustrated Guide to LSTMs and GRUs

research_papers.title: Long Short-Term Memory (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997)

days.expected_outcome: You can model sequential data and understand the motivation for attention.

days.title: Natural Language Processing Fundamentals
days.beginner_explanation: Natural Language Processing lets machines work with human language. Since models need numbers, text must be converted into vectors - from simple counts (bag-of-words, TF-IDF) to dense word embeddings that capture meaning, where similar words sit close together in vector space. These fundamentals underpin everything from search to modern LLMs, giving you the vocabulary to understand what comes next.
days.practical_task: Preprocess a text dataset, build TF-IDF and Word2Vec representations, and train a text classifier comparing both.
days.mini_project: Build a news or review classifier that categorizes text, comparing TF-IDF + logistic regression against an embedding-based approach.
days.assignment: Explain how word embeddings capture semantic meaning better than one-hot or bag-of-words representations.

quiz.question: What do word embeddings provide over one-hot encoding?
TF-IDF gives higher weight to words that are:
options: Frequent in a document but rare across the corpus
quiz.answer: Frequent in a document but rare across the corpus

research_papers.title: Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space (Word2Vec, Mikolov et al., 2013)

days.expected_outcome: You can preprocess text and build fundamental NLP models.

days.title: Transformers and Large Language Models Introduction
days.beginner_explanation: Transformers are the architecture behind virtually all modern AI breakthroughs, from ChatGPT to image generation. Their key innovation, self-attention, lets a model weigh the relevance of every word to every other word in parallel, capturing context far better than RNNs. Large Language Models are transformers trained on massive text corpora, then adapted to tasks. Understanding this conceptually is essential for any AI engineer today.
days.practical_task: Read and run a minimal transformer/attention notebook to see how attention weights connect tokens, then use a pretrained model for a simple task.
days.mini_project: Use a pretrained transformer (via Hugging Face) to build a text summarizer or sentiment analyzer and evaluate its outputs.
days.assignment: In your own words, explain self-attention and why it enables parallel processing of sequences unlike RNNs.

quiz.question: The core innovation of transformers is:
Compared to RNNs, transformers process sequence tokens:

research_papers.title: Attention Is All You Need (Vaswani et al., 2017)

days.expected_outcome: You understand transformers and how modern LLMs work at a conceptual level.

days.title: Hugging Face Ecosystem
days.beginner_explanation: Hugging Face is the GitHub of machine learning models, hosting hundreds of thousands of pretrained models you can use in a few lines of code. The transformers library provides a unified API to load models and tokenizers, and pipelines make common tasks (classification, summarization, QA) trivially easy. Mastering this ecosystem lets you leverage state-of-the-art models without training from scratch.
days.practical_task: Use Hugging Face pipelines for sentiment analysis, summarization, and question answering, then load a model with AutoModel/AutoTokenizer manually.
days.mini_project: Build a multi-task NLP demo app that offers summarization, translation, and sentiment analysis using Hugging Face pipelines.
days.assignment: Explain the difference between using a high-level pipeline and manually loading a model with AutoModel/AutoTokenizer.

quiz.question: What is the Hugging Face Hub?
options: A repository of pretrained models and datasets
quiz.answer: A repository of pretrained models and datasets

quiz.question: A transformers 'pipeline' is:
options: A high-level API for common tasks
quiz.answer: A high-level API for common tasks

research_papers.title: Transformers: State-of-the-Art Natural Language Processing (Wolf et al., 2020)

days.expected_outcome: You can leverage the Hugging Face ecosystem to use state-of-the-art models.

days.title: Transfer Learning and Fine-Tuning
objectives: Know when to fine-tune vs use as-is
days.beginner_explanation: Training large models from scratch requires enormous data and compute. Transfer learning lets you start from a model already trained on huge datasets and adapt it to your specific task with far less data. You can either use it as a fixed feature extractor or fine-tune its weights. This is one of the most practical and powerful techniques in applied AI, and it is how most real projects get built.
days.practical_task: Fine-tune a pretrained transformer (e.g., DistilBERT) on a custom text classification dataset using Hugging Face Trainer.
days.mini_project: Fine-tune a pretrained model (text or image) for a domain-specific task and compare its performance to training from scratch.
days.assignment: Explain the difference between feature extraction and full fine-tuning, and when you would choose each based on data size.

quiz.question: Transfer learning is most useful when:
options: You have unlimited data and compute
You have limited data for your specific task
You never want to reuse models
quiz.answer: You have limited data for your specific task

quiz.question: Freezing early layers during fine-tuning means:
options: Keeping their weights fixed while training later layers
quiz.answer: Keeping their weights fixed while training later layers

research_papers.title: How transferable are features in deep neural networks? (Yosinski et al., 2014)

days.expected_outcome: You can adapt pretrained models to custom tasks with limited data.

days.title: Working with LLM APIs
topics: OpenAI, Anthropic, and other LLM APIs
days.beginner_explanation: Many AI applications are built not by training models but by calling powerful hosted LLMs through APIs. As an AI engineer, you need to call these services securely, control generation with parameters like temperature, count tokens to manage cost, and handle rate limits and errors gracefully. This skill lets you ship intelligent features quickly by standing on the shoulders of frontier models.
days.practical_task: Write a Python script that calls an LLM API, experiments with temperature and max tokens, and streams the response to the console.
days.mini_project: Build a command-line AI assistant that maintains conversation history, manages tokens, and handles API errors and retries gracefully.
days.assignment: Explain how temperature affects output and why you would use low vs high values. Describe how to keep API keys secure.

quiz.question: A higher temperature value generally makes LLM output:
API keys should be stored:
options: In environment variables or a secrets manager
quiz.answer: In environment variables or a secrets manager

youtube.title: OpenAI API Full Course for Beginners

research_papers.title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (GPT-3, Brown et al., 2020)

days.expected_outcome: You can build applications powered by commercial LLM APIs securely and efficiently.

days.title: Prompt Engineering
days.beginner_explanation: Prompt engineering is the skill of communicating with LLMs to get reliable, high-quality results. Small changes in wording, examples, or structure can dramatically change outputs. Techniques like few-shot examples and chain-of-thought reasoning improve accuracy, while requesting structured output (like JSON) makes responses usable in software. It is the fastest lever for improving an AI feature without touching the model.
days.practical_task: Take a task and iteratively improve a prompt using zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought approaches, comparing outputs.
days.mini_project: Build a prompt-based tool (e.g., email drafter or code explainer) that uses system prompts and returns validated structured JSON output.
days.assignment: Demonstrate how few-shot examples and chain-of-thought change the quality of an LLM's answer on a reasoning task.

quiz.question: Chain-of-thought prompting encourages the model to:
Few-shot prompting means:
options: Providing a few examples in the prompt
quiz.answer: Providing a few examples in the prompt

research_papers.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in LLMs (Wei et al., 2022)

days.expected_outcome: You can craft reliable prompts that produce high-quality, usable outputs.

days.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Basics
days.beginner_explanation: LLMs do not know about your private documents and can hallucinate facts. Retrieval-Augmented Generation solves this by retrieving relevant text from your own data and injecting it into the prompt, so answers are grounded in real sources. It combines embeddings, a vector database for similarity search, and an LLM. RAG is the most common architecture for building enterprise AI assistants over private knowledge.
days.practical_task: Build a minimal RAG pipeline: chunk documents, embed them into a vector store, retrieve relevant chunks for a query, and generate a grounded answer.
days.mini_project: Build a 'chat with your documents' app that lets users ask questions about uploaded PDFs and returns answers with source citations.
days.assignment: Explain how RAG reduces hallucination and why document chunking strategy affects retrieval quality.

quiz.question: The main purpose of RAG is to:
options: Ground LLM answers in retrieved external data
quiz.answer: Ground LLM answers in retrieved external data

quiz.question: A vector database is used to:

research_papers.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks (Lewis et al., 2020)

days.expected_outcome: You can build a RAG system that answers questions over your own documents.

days.title: Building AI Applications
objectives: Assemble LLM features into an app
topics: Building a UI with Streamlit or Gradio
days.beginner_explanation: Turning AI capabilities into a real product means wiring together models, data, and a user interface. Frameworks like LangChain help you compose LLM calls, tools, and retrieval into coherent flows, while Streamlit and Gradio let you build interactive UIs in pure Python with minimal effort. This day is about shipping something usable that ties together everything you have learned.
days.practical_task: Build a Streamlit or Gradio app that wraps your RAG pipeline with a chat interface and conversation memory.
days.mini_project: Build and polish a complete AI assistant web app (e.g., a study helper or support bot) combining RAG, memory, and a clean UI.
days.assignment: Sketch the architecture of an AI app you would build, labeling the LLM, retrieval, state, and UI components.

quiz.question: Streamlit and Gradio are used to:
options: Quickly build web UIs in Python
quiz.answer: Quickly build web UIs in Python

quiz.question: LangChain primarily helps you:
options: Compose LLM calls, tools, and retrieval into flows
quiz.answer: Compose LLM calls, tools, and retrieval into flows

research_papers.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models (Yao et al., 2022)

days.expected_outcome: You can build and ship a functional AI application with a user interface.

days.title: Experiment Tracking with MLflow and Weights & Biases
days.beginner_explanation: When you train dozens of models with different settings, memory alone cannot track what worked. Experiment tracking tools like MLflow and Weights & Biases automatically log parameters, metrics, and artifacts, giving you dashboards to compare runs and reproduce results. This discipline is what separates hobby projects from professional ML work, and it is a core part of MLOps.
days.practical_task: Instrument a training script with MLflow or W&B to log hyperparameters and metrics across several runs, then compare them in the dashboard.
days.mini_project: Run a hyperparameter sweep on a model, track all runs with W&B or MLflow, and produce a report identifying the best configuration.
days.assignment: Explain why experiment tracking is essential for reproducibility, and list what you should log for every training run.

quiz.question: Experiment tracking tools primarily help with:
options: Logging and comparing training runs reproducibly
quiz.answer: Logging and comparing training runs reproducibly

quiz.question: A model registry is used to:

research_papers.title: Hidden Technical Debt in Machine Learning Systems (Sculley et al., 2015)

days.expected_outcome: You can systematically track, compare, and reproduce ML experiments.

days.title: Model Deployment with FastAPI
days.beginner_explanation: A model is only useful when others can call it. FastAPI is a modern, high-performance Python framework for building REST APIs, ideal for serving ML models. You load your trained model once, expose a prediction endpoint, validate inputs with Pydantic, and return results as JSON. This is the standard way to put a model behind an interface that web and mobile apps can consume.
days.practical_task: Wrap a trained model in a FastAPI app with a /predict endpoint, validate inputs with Pydantic, and test it with sample requests.
days.mini_project: Deploy one of your earlier models as a FastAPI service with input validation, error handling, and interactive docs, tested end to end.
days.assignment: Explain why input validation with Pydantic matters for a prediction API and what could go wrong without it.

quiz.question: FastAPI is used to:
options: Build REST APIs to serve models
quiz.answer: Build REST APIs to serve models

quiz.question: Pydantic in FastAPI provides:

research_papers.title: Hidden Technical Debt in Machine Learning Systems (Sculley et al., 2015)

days.expected_outcome: You can serve trained models through a production-style REST API.

days.title: Containerization with Docker
days.beginner_explanation: It works on my machine is a classic problem. Docker solves it by packaging your app and all its dependencies into a portable container that runs identically anywhere. For ML, this means your model, code, and exact library versions travel together, making deployment reliable. Docker is a foundational DevOps and MLOps skill and the standard unit of deployment in the cloud.
days.practical_task: Write a Dockerfile for your FastAPI model service, build the image, and run the container locally serving predictions.
days.mini_project: Containerize a full ML app (model API plus a Streamlit UI) using Docker Compose so it starts with a single command.
days.assignment: Explain the difference between a container and a VM, and why containers are lightweight and reproducible.

quiz.question: A Docker container packages:
options: The app plus its dependencies and environment
quiz.answer: The app plus its dependencies and environment

quiz.question: Compared to a VM, a container:
options: Shares the host kernel and is more lightweight
quiz.answer: Shares the host kernel and is more lightweight

research_papers.title: Machine Learning Operations (MLOps): Overview, Definition, and Architecture (Kreuzberger et al., 2022)

days.expected_outcome: You can package ML applications into portable Docker containers.

days.title: Introduction to MLOps
days.beginner_explanation: MLOps applies DevOps principles to machine learning, addressing the unique challenges of data and models that change over time. It covers versioning data and models, building reproducible pipelines, automating training and deployment, and monitoring in production. Understanding the MLOps lifecycle helps you build systems that are reliable, reproducible, and maintainable rather than one-off notebooks that break.
days.practical_task: Set up DVC to version a dataset and model alongside Git, and document a reproducible pipeline from data to trained model.
days.mini_project: Create a reproducible ML project skeleton with versioned data (DVC), tracked experiments, and a documented pipeline others can rerun.
days.assignment: Explain how MLOps differs from traditional DevOps, focusing on the added challenges of data and model drift.

quiz.question: MLOps adds which concerns beyond traditional DevOps?
options: Data and model versioning, drift, and retraining
quiz.answer: Data and model versioning, drift, and retraining

quiz.question: DVC is primarily used to:
options: Version data and models with Git
quiz.answer: Version data and models with Git

research_papers.title: Machine Learning Operations (MLOps): Overview, Definition, and Architecture (Kreuzberger et al., 2022)

days.expected_outcome: You understand the MLOps lifecycle and can version data and models.

days.title: CI/CD for Machine Learning
days.beginner_explanation: Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment automate the path from code change to running system. For ML, CI/CD tests your code and data, retrains or validates models, and deploys them automatically when checks pass. GitHub Actions is a popular tool to define these workflows. Automation reduces human error and lets you ship improvements safely and frequently instead of through risky manual steps.
days.practical_task: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that runs tests and builds a Docker image for your model API on every push.
days.mini_project: Set up an end-to-end CI/CD pipeline that tests, builds, and deploys your containerized model API automatically on push to main.
days.assignment: Describe what a CI/CD pipeline for an ML project should test and automate, from code commit to deployment.

quiz.question: CI/CD primarily aims to:
options: Automate testing and deployment for safe, frequent releases
quiz.answer: Automate testing and deployment for safe, frequent releases

quiz.question: GitHub Actions is used to:

research_papers.title: Machine Learning Operations (MLOps): Overview, Definition, and Architecture (Kreuzberger et al., 2022)

days.expected_outcome: You can automate testing and deployment of ML applications with CI/CD.

days.title: Cloud Platforms for AI
objectives: Deploy a model to the cloud
topics: Cloud storage for data and models
Deploying a container to the cloud
days.beginner_explanation: Production AI usually runs in the cloud, which offers on-demand compute, GPUs, storage, and managed ML services. The big three (AWS, GCP, Azure) each provide platforms like SageMaker or Vertex AI that handle training, deployment, and scaling. You do not need to master every service, but you should be able to deploy a containerized model, use cloud storage, and understand cost implications of your choices.
days.practical_task: Deploy your Dockerized model API to a cloud service (e.g., a container service or a managed endpoint) and call it over the internet.
days.mini_project: Deploy a complete AI app to the cloud with proper storage and a public endpoint, and document the setup and estimated costs.
days.assignment: Compare the three major cloud providers' AI offerings at a high level and describe when you would use a managed ML service.

quiz.question: Managed ML services like SageMaker/Vertex AI primarily:
options: Handle training, deployment, and scaling for you
quiz.answer: Handle training, deployment, and scaling for you

quiz.question: A key reason to use the cloud for AI is:
options: On-demand compute and GPUs that scale
It removes the need for models
quiz.answer: On-demand compute and GPUs that scale

research_papers.title: Hidden Technical Debt in Machine Learning Systems (Sculley et al., 2015)

days.expected_outcome: You can deploy AI applications to the cloud using managed services.

days.title: Model Monitoring and Maintenance
days.beginner_explanation: A deployed model is not done - the world changes, and model performance drifts as incoming data diverges from training data. Monitoring tracks prediction quality, input distributions, and system health, alerting you when things degrade. Detecting data drift and concept drift lets you retrain before users are affected. Building feedback loops turns real-world usage into data that keeps your model accurate over time.
days.practical_task: Add logging and a simple drift check to a deployed model, simulating drifted input and detecting the change.
days.mini_project: Build a monitoring dashboard for a deployed model that tracks prediction distributions and flags drift with alerts.
days.assignment: Explain the difference between data drift and concept drift with an example of each, and how you would respond to them.

quiz.question: Concept drift occurs when:
options: The relationship between inputs and target changes over time
quiz.answer: The relationship between inputs and target changes over time

quiz.question: Why monitor a model after deployment?
options: Performance can degrade as data drifts
quiz.answer: Performance can degrade as data drifts

research_papers.title: A Survey on Concept Drift Adaptation (Gama et al., 2014)

days.expected_outcome: You can monitor deployed models and respond to performance degradation.

days.title: Data Engineering Basics for AI
objectives: Work with batch and streaming data
days.beginner_explanation: AI systems are only as good as the data feeding them, and that data rarely arrives clean and ready. Data engineering builds the pipelines that collect, transform, and deliver data reliably. Understanding ETL, batch vs streaming, storage options like warehouses and lakes, and orchestration tools like Airflow helps you ensure your models always have fresh, high-quality data - a skill increasingly expected of AI engineers.
days.practical_task: Build a simple ETL pipeline in Python that extracts data from a source, transforms it, and loads it into a database or file store.
days.mini_project: Create a scheduled data pipeline (with Airflow or a simple scheduler) that ingests and cleans data daily to feed an ML model.
days.assignment: Explain the difference between batch and streaming data processing and give a use case suited to each.

quiz.question: ETL stands for:
Apache Airflow is used for:

research_papers.title: The Dataflow Model (Akidau et al., 2015)

days.expected_outcome: You can build basic data pipelines that reliably feed ML systems.

days.title: Responsible AI and Ethics
days.beginner_explanation: As AI makes decisions affecting people's lives, building it responsibly is not optional. Models can inherit and amplify bias from data, leading to unfair outcomes. Explainability tools like SHAP and LIME help you understand why a model made a decision, while privacy techniques protect sensitive data. Understanding fairness, transparency, and emerging regulation makes you a trustworthy engineer who builds systems people can rely on.
days.practical_task: Use SHAP or LIME to explain a model's predictions, and audit a model for bias across a sensitive attribute.
days.mini_project: Build a model fairness and explainability report for one of your earlier models, identifying any bias and proposing mitigations.
days.assignment: Describe a real scenario where ML bias caused harm, and outline steps you would take to detect and mitigate it.

quiz.question: SHAP and LIME are tools for:
ML models can be biased because:
options: They learn patterns, including biases, from training data
quiz.answer: They learn patterns, including biases, from training data

youtube.title: AI Ethics and Responsible AI Explained

research_papers.title: A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions (SHAP, Lundberg & Lee, 2017)

days.expected_outcome: You can build and audit AI systems for fairness, transparency, and privacy.

days.title: Capstone Project: Build and Deploy an End-to-End AI System
objectives: Integrate the full AI engineering workflow
days.beginner_explanation: The capstone ties everything together into one portfolio-worthy project. You will choose a real problem, gather and prepare data, train and evaluate a model (classical ML or an LLM-powered feature), then serve it via a FastAPI API, containerize it with Docker, deploy it to the cloud, and add basic monitoring and CI/CD. Completing a full end-to-end system is the single most convincing proof that you can work as an AI engineer.
days.practical_task: Select a problem, build a working end-to-end solution from data to deployed model, and verify it runs in the cloud with a public endpoint.
days.mini_project: Complete a full capstone: an end-to-end AI application (e.g., a document assistant, recommendation service, or image classifier) deployed and accessible online with monitoring.
days.assignment: Write project documentation covering the problem, data, model choices, architecture, deployment, and known limitations.

quiz.question: A strong AI engineering capstone should demonstrate:
options: The full workflow from data to deployed, monitored system
quiz.answer: The full workflow from data to deployed, monitored system

quiz.question: Why include monitoring in your capstone?
options: To show you can maintain a model in production
quiz.answer: To show you can maintain a model in production

youtube.title: End to End Machine Learning Project

research_papers.title: Machine Learning Operations (MLOps): Overview, Definition, and Architecture (Kreuzberger et al., 2022)

days.expected_outcome: You have a complete, deployed AI system that showcases your full skill set.

days.title: Portfolio, Career Preparation, and Next Steps
topics: Writing project READMEs and case studies
Resume and LinkedIn for AI roles
days.beginner_explanation: Skills get you hired only if others can see them. This final day focuses on presenting your work: a clean GitHub portfolio with well-documented projects, a resume and LinkedIn tuned for AI roles, and interview preparation covering ML concepts, coding, and system design. You will also map out how to keep learning and choose a specialization. The learning never stops in AI, but you will finish with a clear direction and momentum.
days.practical_task: Polish your GitHub, write strong READMEs for your projects, and update your resume/LinkedIn to highlight AI engineering skills.
days.mini_project: Assemble a portfolio website or curated GitHub profile featuring your capstone and 2-3 best projects with clear write-ups.
days.assignment: Draft a 60-second self-introduction for an AI engineer interview and list 10 concepts you would review before interviewing.

quiz.question: The most convincing item in an AI engineer portfolio is usually:
options: Deployed end-to-end projects with clear documentation
quiz.answer: Deployed end-to-end projects with clear documentation

quiz.question: Why keep learning after this roadmap?
options: The field evolves rapidly with new models and tools
quiz.answer: The field evolves rapidly with new models and tools

youtube.title: How to Build a Data Science / AI Portfolio

research_papers.title: Deep Learning (LeCun, Bengio & Hinton, 2015)

days.expected_outcome: You have a job-ready portfolio and a clear plan to launch your AI engineer career.
